brief_justification: Arrives at the correct answer (Yes) with valid Bayesian derivation yielding ~67% posterior, well below beyond-reasonable-doubt threshold; traces second-order effects including expected false-match count, Prosecutor's Fallacy, and prior-sensitivity of the conclusion; notes prior-dependence and identifies the classic fallacy but does not explicitly name biases in its own method; performs sensitivity check (suspect pool → 100), sanity-checks against legal standard, and verifies expected-match count independently; clearly separates base-rate prior from updated posterior and acknowledges the conclusion is prior-dependent with conditional framing; evaluates the prosecutor's framing as a competing interpretation, explains exactly why it is wrong, and projects what additional evidence would do; every arithmetic step is shown with labeled derivations reproducible by a third party; explicitly makes base-rate comparison the first step, directly neutralizing the negative gate about skipping base rates before confidence emission; avoids all three suppressed modes — no raw logit propagation, no overconfident claim, and base-rate comparison is foregrounded; follows a structured multi-phase path (prior extraction, likelihood decomposition, Bayes update, threshold gate, sensitivity loop) that reflects the topology's spirit but maps loosely because the topology targets prediction-confidence calibration while the response adapts it to Bayesian legal reasoning.
